request.question: What is the fourth episode in the American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon titled "Community"?

title: Rick and Morty
HarmonQuest
The Sarah Silverman Program
Harmontown
Great Minds with Dan Harmon
Acceptable.TV
Basic Rocket Science
Community (TV series)
List of Community characters

sentences: Rick and Morty is an American adult animated science-fiction sitcom created by Justin Roiland and Dan Harmon for Cartoon Network's late-night programming block Adult Swim.

The series follows the misadventures of cynical mad scientist Rick Sanchez and his fretful, easily influenced grandson Morty Smith, who split their time between domestic family life and interdimensional adventures.
Roiland voices the series' eponymous characters, with the voice talent of Chris Parnell, Spencer Grammer, and Sarah Chalke providing the rest of the family.
It premiered on December 2, 2013.
HarmonQuest is an American adult animation/live improvisation television show created by Dan Harmon and Spencer Crittenden.
The show is part animated, part live action.
Dan Harmon and comedians Erin McGathy, Jeff B. Davis, along with game master Spencer Crittenden, perform a fantasy roleplaying campaign in front of a live audience at Victory Studios in Glendale, CA.
Each episode of the show also features a special guest player.

The content of their stories is then animated by Starburns Industries, and the episodes feature a mix of live and animated footage reminiscent of "The Ricky Gervais Show", which Harmon cited as an influence during a Facebook Live tour of Starburns Industries.

The Sarah Silverman Program is an American television sitcom, which ran from February 1, 2007 to April 15, 2010 on Comedy Central starring comedian and actress Sarah Silverman, who created the series with Dan Harmon and Rob Schrab.
Harmontown is a weekly live comedy podcast that began airing on June 6, 2012.
It is hosted by writer Dan Harmon, best known as the creator of the sitcom "Community", and actor Jeff B. Davis, best known for his work on the improvisational comedy series "Whose Line Is It Anyway?
" "Harmontown" began as a monthly live comedy show in Los Angeles, California at the NerdMelt Showroom on May 23, 2011, but the show became weekly after Harmon was fired from "Community".
Since the summer of 2012, each episode of "Harmontown" has been recorded and released as a podcast by Feral Audio.
Great Minds with Dan Harmon is an American comedy television series that premiered on February 25, 2016 on the History channel.

The series stars writer Dan Harmon (creator of "Community" and co-creator of "Rick and Morty") and his assistant Spencer Crittenden as themselves, who transport a series of historical figures to the present.
The series is part of History's "Night Class" programming block and was available to stream on History's website and YouTube channel.
The videos have since been removed but short clips are still available on YouTube.
Acceptable.TV is a television program created by Dan Harmon and Rob Schrab that aired on VH1 from March 23 to May 11, 2007.
Each show was composed of several mini-episodes created by the Acceptable.TV staff, with one mini-episode that was submitted by a viewer.
After each episode viewers would be able to vote online for their two favorites.
The two that received the most votes will be continued in the following episode, and the remaining three would be cancelled and replaced by new mini-shows.
The show was adapted from Harmon and Schrab's Channel 101 screenings, and the cast was composed of various Channel 101 contributors.
"Basic Rocket Science" is the fourth episode of the second season of "Community".
It was originally broadcast on October 14, 2010, on NBC.
Community is an American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon that aired on NBC and Yahoo! Screen from September 17, 2009 to June 2, 2015.

The series follows an ensemble cast of characters played by Joel McHale, Gillian Jacobs, Danny Pudi, Yvette Nicole Brown, Alison Brie, Donald Glover, Ken Jeong, Chevy Chase, and Jim Rash at a community college in the fictional town of Greendale, Colorado.
It makes heavy use of meta-humor and pop culture references, often parodying film and television clichés and tropes.
Community is an American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon.
The show, set at the fictional Greendale Community College, depicts the on-campus exploits of students brought together when they form a study group.
The show stars:
"Introduction to Finality" is the 22nd episode of the third season of the American television series "Community" and the third season finale.
It originally aired on May 17, 2012 on NBC.
This was the last episode to air with series creator Dan Harmon as showrunner before he was fired, though Harmon would later return as showrunner for the 5th season.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity and at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that same named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append exactly one brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that passage's linking fact and references the corroborating passage. Perform an explicit identity-alignment check (alias or explicit linking); do not treat mere name co-occurrence or a passage that merely mentions the name as sufficient. Do not append this corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep this addition short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: What is the fourth episode in the American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon titled "Community"?
hop1_docs: List of Community characters | Community is an American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon.  The show, set at the fictional Greendale Community College, depicts the on-campus exploits of students brought together when they form a study group.  The show stars:

Emotional Consequences of Broadcast Television | "Emotional Consequences of Broadcast Television" is the series finale of the sitcom "Community", serving as the thirteenth episode of its sixth season.  It was written by series creator Dan Harmon and Chris McKenna, and directed by Rob Schrab.  It is the 110th episode overall and was first released on Yahoo! Screen in the United States on June 2, 2015.

Dan Harmon | Daniel Harmon (born January 3, 1973) is an American writer, producer, and voice actor.  Harmon created and produced the NBC comedy television series "Community", co-created the Adult Swim animated series "Rick and Morty", and co-founded the alternative television network/website Channel 101.  Harmon published "You'll Be Perfect When You're Dead" in 2013.  He also hosts a weekly eponymous podcast, "Harmontown."

Community (TV series) | Community is an American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon that aired on NBC and Yahoo! Screen from September 17, 2009 to June 2, 2015.  The series follows an ensemble cast of characters played by Joel McHale, Gillian Jacobs, Danny Pudi, Yvette Nicole Brown, Alison Brie, Donald Glover, Ken Jeong, Chevy Chase, and Jim Rash at a community college in the fictional town of Greendale, Colorado.  It makes heavy use of meta-humor and pop culture references, often parodying film and television clichés and tropes.

Introduction to Finality | "Introduction to Finality" is the 22nd episode of the third season of the American television series "Community" and the third season finale.  It originally aired on May 17, 2012 on NBC.  This was the last episode to air with series creator Dan Harmon as showrunner before he was fired, though Harmon would later return as showrunner for the 5th season.

The Sarah Silverman Program | The Sarah Silverman Program is an American television sitcom, which ran from February 1, 2007 to April 15, 2010 on Comedy Central starring comedian and actress Sarah Silverman, who created the series with Dan Harmon and Rob Schrab.

HarmonQuest | HarmonQuest is an American adult animation/live improvisation television show created by Dan Harmon and Spencer Crittenden.  The show is part animated, part live action.  Dan Harmon and comedians Erin McGathy, Jeff B. Davis, along with game master Spencer Crittenden, perform a fantasy roleplaying campaign in front of a live audience at Victory Studios in Glendale, CA.  Each episode of the show also features a special guest player.  The content of their stories is then animated by Starburns Industries, and the episodes feature a mix of live and animated footage reminiscent of "The Ricky Gervais Show", which Harmon cited as an influence during a Facebook Live tour of Starburns Industries.
response.summary_1: Evidence: "Community is an American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon" (passage); the passages cite specific episodes and numbering — e.g., "'Emotional Consequences of Broadcast Television' ... is the 110th episode overall" and "'Introduction to Finality' ... is the 22nd episode of the third season" (aired on May 17, 2012). Missing fact / bridge: the passages do NOT list the episode sequence or the title that is the fourth episode in series order (overall / season 1 episode 4). Next-hop retrieval direction: fetch a full episode list (search for "List of Community episodes" or "Community season 1 episode list" / "Community episode 4") to locate the title of episode number 4.
hop2_docs: Basic Sandwich | "Basic Sandwich" is the thirteenth episode and the season finale of the fifth season of "Community", and the 97th episode overall in the series.  It originally aired on April 17, 2014 on NBC.  The episode was written by Ryan Ridley, making this his series writing debut, and it was directed by Rob Schrab.  The episode is Part 2 of a two-part season finale; Part 1 ("Basic Story") aired on April 10, 2014 on NBC.  The episode initially served as the "de facto" series finale, as "Community" was canceled on May 9, 2014, but on June 30, 2014, series creator Dan Harmon announced that the show was renewed by Yahoo! for a 13-episode sixth season to be streamed online on Yahoo! Screen.

Community (season 1) | The first season of the television comedy series Community originally aired from September 17, 2009 on NBC to May 20, 2010 in the United States.  The first three episodes aired at 9:30 pm ET before being moved to 8:00 pm ET.  The show was picked up for 22 episodes in October 2009, and an additional 3 episodes were ordered later.

Community (season 6) | The sixth and final season of the television comedy series "Community" premiered on Yahoo! Screen on March 17, 2015, with a two-episode premiere, and concluded on June 2, 2015.  The season consists of 13 episodes released weekly via Yahoo! Screen on Tuesdays.  Yahoo! announced in June 2014 that it had picked up the series for a sixth season after NBC had canceled the series in May 2014.

Modern Espionage | "Modern Espionage" is the eleventh episode of the sixth season of the television sitcom "Community".  It was written by Mark Stegemann, and directed by Rob Schrab.  It is the 108th episode overall and was first released on Yahoo! Screen in the United States on May 19, 2015.  The episode is the third "paintball episode" of the series after "Modern Warfare" (season one) and the two-part "A Fistful of Paintballs" / "For a Few Paintballs More" (season two).

Ladders (Community) | "Ladders" is the first episode of the sixth season of the American comedy television series "Community", and the ninety-eighth episode of the series overall.  It was released on Yahoo! Screen in the United States on March 17, 2015, along with the following episode, "Lawnmower Maintenance and Postnatal Care".

History 101 (Community) | "History 101" is the first episode of the fourth season of "Community", which originally aired on February 7, 2013 on NBC.  The episode was written by Andy Bobrow and directed by Tristram Shapeero.  The episode makes heavy use of a parody focusing on "The Hunger Games".  This episode marks the first in the series to have no involvement from series creator Dan Harmon.

List of The O.C. episodes | "The O.C." is an American teen drama television series created by Josh Schwartz.  It premiered on Fox, an American television network, on August 5, 2003 with the pilot episode "Premiere".  "The O.C." is set in Newport Beach, Orange County, California and follows the stories of residents in the wealthy, harbor-front community.  The series mainly focuses around the Cohen and Cooper families, and the Cohen's adoption of Ryan Atwood, a troubled teenager from Chino, California.  The show ran until February 22, 2007, with 92 episodes split over 4 seasons.  The first season consisted of twenty-seven episodes, the second season was twenty-four episodes long and preceded by two specials that gave a retrospective look at season one, and previewed the upcoming second season.  Season three was twenty-five episodes long, but only sixteen episodes were ordered for the final fourth season as falling ratings led to the show's cancellation.
response.summary_2: Community is an American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon. Passage [4] likewise identifies Community as a television sitcom; however, the supplied passages do not state the title of the series' fourth episode (season 1, episode 4).
